metadata.purpose: Lichen records kept separately from mushroom records.
metadata.organization_rule: Physical file placement follows stable biological or morphological identity. Useful parts, routes, safety, and seasonal behavior remain record-level fields.

records.culinary_uses: Emergency/survival-use lichen only: historical use involved soaking/boiling or alkaline processing. Not recommended as a routine edible.
records.edibility_detail: Not a normal wild food in this guide. Historically used as famine/survival food only after extensive processing to reduce bitterness and acids; avoid casual eating.

mushroom_profile.summary: A lichen rather than a mushroom. Historically processed as famine food or flour extender, but bitter acids require substantial preparation.
host_trees: None; may occur in boreal/open pine woods but does not fruit from wood
mushroom_profile.season_note: Visible year-round; slow-growing and persistent rather than a short mushroom flush.
research_notes: Better treated as a lichen entry than a mushroom entry.
Known more as animal browse and famine food than as a casual wild edible.
Source coverage count in current app data: 2
fertile_surface: Lichen thallus; no mushroom gills or pores

records.food_quality: Emergency / survival history only; not routine food
records.field_identification: Pale gray-white, highly branched fruticose lichen forming dry cushiony mats on the ground, not a moss and not a gilled mushroom. Branches repeatedly divide into small forked tips; look for open, well-drained boreal pine woods, barrens, heaths, sandy soil, humus, or soil over rock. Slow growth and lichen ecology are key ID/use clues; harvest pressure should be low.

field_measurements.overall: Mats/cushions up to about 10 cm tall / up to about 4 in tall
field_measurements.thickness: Thicker branches usually about 1–1.5 mm wide / about 1/32–1/16 in wide
field_measurements.notes: Moister sites may produce taller podetia to roughly 12 cm / about 4.7 in; drier sites often shorter, about 4–6 cm / 1.6–2.4 in.

records.other_uses: Emergency / survival context, reindeer/caribou forage, and occasional decorative/ecology interest; slow growth makes heavy harvesting a bad idea.
completion_gaps: Record is a lichen-group entry, not a single casual food mushroom; edibility remains emergency-only.

records.mushroom_card_note: Reindeer lichen; emergency/survival-history and ecology entry, not routine food.

records.medicinal_uses: Traditional source of vitamin C in survival situations
records.culinary_uses: Emergency food only when properly processed; not a normal table mushroom.
records.edibility_detail: Survival/emergency-food history only; not recommended as a routine edible. Processing/boiling is needed in traditional accounts and heavy harvesting of slow-growing lichens is poor practice.
records.scientific_name: Umbilicaria spp.; representative detail from Umbilicaria mammulata

mushroom_profile.summary: Actually a rock-dwelling lichen rather than a mushroom. Historically used as an emergency or famine food after extensive processing.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Visible year-round where established; not a short fruiting-season mushroom.
research_notes: Common name covers edible Umbilicaria lichens rather than a single mushroom species.
Treat as a survival food entry, not a routine edible.
Slow-growing and best not heavily harvested.
Source coverage count in current app data: 2
fertile_surface: Lichen thallus; no mushroom gills or pores

records.food_quality: Emergency / survival history only; not routine food
records.field_identification: Large, leathery foliose lichen attached to rock by a central holdfast/navel-like point, usually forming dark, rounded, leaf-like thalli on boulders or steep rock walls. Upper surface is reddish-brown to gray-brown; underside can be dark to black. ID depends on rock substrate, central attachment, leathery thallus, and lichen—not mushroom—structure.

field_measurements.overall: Representative U. mammulata thallus usually about 4–15 cm across / about 1.6–5.9 in across
field_measurements.notes: Exceptional U. mammulata specimens reported to 63 cm / about 2.1 ft across. This guide entry covers rock-tripe lichens broadly, so the range is representative, not every Umbilicaria species.

records.other_uses: Emergency / survival context and ID/lichen ecology entry. Slow-growing rock lichens should be observed more than harvested.
completion_gaps: Record is a broad rock-tripe lichen group; measurements use U. mammulata as representative rather than every Umbilicaria species.

records.mushroom_card_note: Rock-dwelling lichen; emergency/survival-history entry, not a routine edible mushroom.
